preFix.title: Making a potting mix from finished compost
preFix.subtitle: Blending garden compost with sharp sand and grit to make a functional growing medium
preFix.excerpt: Finished garden compost is too rich and moisture-retentive to use alone as potting compost. Mixed with sharp sand and perlite or grit, it becomes a functional and free growing medium for most vegetable seedlings and container growing. This covers the ratios for different uses.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS guidance on homemade potting compost. Garden Organic advice on compost-based growing media. Charles Dowding no-dig growing media discussions.

content.text: Garden compost has higher electrical conductivity (soluble salts) than commercial potting compost, and a nutrient-release rate that can overwhelm seedlings. It also holds more moisture than container roots prefer. A blended potting mix dilutes the compost with inert materials to give the right drainage, aeration, and nutrient level.

Seed sowing: 1 part finished compost : 2 parts sharp sand. Sieve the compost through a 5 mm sieve first to remove lumps and weed seeds. This produces a fine, free-draining medium. Nutrients are low, which prevents 'damping off' from over-rich conditions.

Potting on and container growing: 2 parts finished compost : 1 part sharp sand : 1 part perlite (or grit). The perlite improves aeration; the compost provides nutrients for 4 to 6 weeks of growth; the sand improves drainage. Suitable for tomatoes, peppers, herbs, leafy veg.

Raised beds: 50% finished compost : 30% topsoil : 20% sharp sand or horticultural grit. This is closer to a soil blend than a potting compost and supports perennial crops and raised-bed production well.
Ericaceous blend (acid-lovers: blueberries, rhododendrons): compost is typically pH 6.5 to 7.5 and too alkaline. Use
from oak or beech leaves in place of compost (pH 4.5 to 5.5), or add sulphur chips to lower the compost pH.
Smell: finished compost smells earthy. If it smells of ammonia or has a sour fermented smell, it is not sufficiently mature. Allow another 4 to 6 weeks of curing in a covered heap before using.

Weed seed test: fill a small pot with the compost and leave in a warm spot for two weeks. Count the weed seedlings that emerge. Fewer than 5 per pot of 500 ml is acceptable for general use; zero is preferable for seed-sowing mixes (this indicates the compost reached 55°C+ consistently).
Texture: good finished compost has a crumbly, open texture. If it feels slimy or compact and clogs the sieve, it needs more time or more turning.
Limitations of home compost potting mixes

Home compost varies significantly between batches. A sowing mix made from compost that had a lot of grass clippings will be richer than one from a woodier batch. This variation means the ratios above are starting points, not guarantees. For high-value seedlings or plants with precise nutrient needs, a small test batch (fill two or three pots and observe seedling growth over 2 weeks) before committing to a full tray is worthwhile. Commercial peat-free composts are more consistent but home compost mixes are good enough for most vegetable growing and have zero financial cost once the compost is produced.
